main_summary.summary: Rev. Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker, interviewed June 20, 2014 in Chester, Virginia by David Cline, recounts his trajectory from a New Jersey preacher's-kid childhood through eight years as pastor of Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg, Virginia, to his service as SCLC executive director under Martin Luther King Jr. Walker describes integrating the Petersburg ministerial alliance and the Petersburg public library (February 27, 1960), which he characterizes as the first library desegregated in the South, and the arrest that followed. He details building SCLC from a $57,000 budget and one office into a $1,000,000 budget and 100 staff, his design and execution of Project C in Birmingham, the year of advance planning before demonstrations, the strategic use of the Huntley-Brinkley evening news, the typing of King's Letter from the Birmingham Jail from marginalia on newspapers and toilet paper, and his role as Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington. Theresa Walker joins to recount the Mother's Day 1963 Birmingham assault and a subsequent arrest with four children in East Point, Georgia. Walker also describes his Harlem pastorate at Canaan Baptist Church, anti-apartheid organizing tied to the ANC, and charter school advocacy in New York with Rev. Floyd H. Flake.
key_themes: Project C and the Birmingham campaign
Letter from Birmingham Jail transcription and naming
Black church infrastructure as movement foundation
main_summary.historical_significance:
Walker's testimony is a first-person operational account from inside SCLC's executive leadership during the campaigns that produced the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965. He provides direct attribution of Project C's design, the year of advance reconnaissance in Birmingham (counting eating-facility seats, timing walks by age cohort), the deliberate cultivation of Bull Connor's overreaction, and the choreography of the children's marches and fire-hose footage to reach the Huntley-Brinkley audience.

The interview preserves Walker's claim of authorship-by-transcription on Letter from the Birmingham Jail and his naming of the document, his pre-King fantasy of plastic-bomb retaliation against Theodore Bilbo and James Eastland, and Theresa Walker's eyewitness account of the Mother's Day 1963 Gaston Motel violence. Walker's late-career anti-apartheid mobilization and charter school activism extend the record beyond 1968.

chapters.part: A Preacher's Kid in Little Georgia
chapters.title: Chief of Staff to Dr. King
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Chester, Virginia, on June 20, 2014, the Reverend Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker says his one claim to fame is having been chief of staff to Martin Luther King Jr., and describes growing up a preacher's kid whose father pastored a small South Jersey congregation, read Greek and Hebrew daily, and at first opposed his joining King and leaving the Petersburg church.

chapters.part: A Preacher's Kid in Little Georgia
chapters.title: You're Colored
chapters.summary: Walker recalls losing his first girlfriend, a neighbor named Nancy who said she could not play with him because he was colored, attending segregated schools until junior high, the Ku Klux Klan burning a cross at a Father Divine headquarters, and a father who was a race man with a portrait of Frederick Douglass on the wall, in a part of North South Jersey so bad they called it Little Georgia.

chapters.part: A Preacher's Kid in Little Georgia
chapters.title: The Freest Man in Society
chapters.summary: Walker explains that though he majored in physics and chemistry and wanted to be a physician, his experiences with segregation in Richmond made the call to ministry more pressing, that he saw the Black preacher as the freest man in society because accountable to the Black community, and that as a Harlem pastor he marched against the drug traffic and became New York State's largest developer of affordable housing.

chapters.title: Twenty-Two Years Old at Gillfield
chapters.summary: Walker recounts attending Virginia Union University and its graduate school of religion for his MDiv, being called to the Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg in his last year at age twenty-two, discovering only recently that his birth year was 1928 rather than 1929, serving Petersburg eight years, then moving to New York as a publishing marketing director before pastoring in Harlem for thirty-seven years.

chapters.title: Through the White Door
chapters.summary: Walker describes integrating the segregated Petersburg ministerial alliance with the chaplain of Virginia State and pastor R.G. Williams, then leading a demonstration on February 27, 1960, after the city council refused to integrate the library, when he and Williams went through the white door, were arrested, and chose to stay in jail for a couple of days, in what he says was the first library desegregated in the South.

chapters.title: I Put My Gun Down
chapters.summary: Walker recalls asking for Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee, whom he thought guilty of treason, to see why the South made Lee a hero, and how he had known King since about 1952 when he joined the board and did press releases; he tells how he carried a nickel-plated Smith & Wesson .32 to Petersburg until King's influence made him put the gun down, an inherited rage tracing to his father, who in a whites-only train station near Norfolk gripped his pistol against a constable, and how he had fantasized in college about plastic bombs aimed at segregationists like Bilbo and Eastland before becoming King's right-hand man in nonviolence.

chapters.title: An Idea in Dr. King's Briefcase
chapters.topic: Growing the SCLC as Executive Director
chapters.summary: Walker recounts joining the SCLC at the Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting in 1958 and becoming Executive Director in 1960 after Ella Baker's interim tenure, finding the organization mainly an idea in King's briefcase and installing a fundraising system that grew the budget from $57,000 to over a million dollars and the staff to a hundred full-time employees; he calls money the ammunition needed to train people in the novel concept of nonviolence and notes that behind the movement in the streets lay enormous planning, with Birmingham started a year ahead of time.

chapters.title: Too Many Targets in Albany
chapters.summary: Walker argues that Albany, considered a failure by the media, was in his view a success despite mistakes, hampered by too many targets and by a campaign initiated by SNCC workers that exhausted the community, leading SNCC to invite King in; he describes sharp organizational competition among SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and the SCLC, journalist Louis Lomax claiming King left with his tail between his legs, and the lasting lesson of how to mobilize an entire community.

chapters.title: I Raised Him in My Congregation
chapters.summary: Walker says he raised SNCC's Charles Sherrod in his Petersburg congregation and got a Woolworth grant to help finance Sherrod's education, and recalls Sherrod telling how, in a Washington meeting where Robert Kennedy pressed the movement toward voter registration over direct action, Sherrod grew so angry he nearly took a swing at the Attorney General, Walker insisting the protest marches and direct action were what made the movement.

chapters.title: Project C for Confrontation
chapters.summary: Walker explains that the main lesson carried from Albany was how to organize an entire community by moving mass meetings from place to place, sustained in Birmingham for forty-three nights despite a bus strike, and describes being assigned by King to build the strategy, going a year ahead to measure how long it took people to reach the stores and count seats, producing Project C, which stood for confrontation of the evil of segregation, with the expectation that Bull Connor would react in a way that helped them, which his resistance to letting marchers go downtown did.

chapters.title: The Children's March
chapters.summary: Walker recalls that the nonviolent credo allowed anyone to join, that Al Hibbler was arrested in Birmingham, and that he, King, and James Bevel decided to use the children despite expected criticism, because the sight of firemen turning hoses on children running down the sidewalk made the nation say this will not do, leading a Mississippi physician to declare that segregation was over.

chapters.title: I Got It
chapters.summary: Walker describes deliberately working to get three or four minutes on the Huntley-Brinkley report, realizing while a thousand onlookers gathered for a delayed march that slowing the demonstrations would draw the crowd and the cameras, so that when Bull Connor put the dogs and water hoses on them and they made the broadcast several nights in a row, he felt they were home free, the use of media being a key to Birmingham's success.

chapters.title: Beaten on Mother's Day
chapters.summary: Walker explains that the NAACP, outlawed in Alabama and reliant on the courts, lacked an action program, that winning integration through courts alone would take a hundred years, and recounts the personal cost: his wife assaulted by an Alabama state trooper with a rifle, and himself beaten on Mother's Day with a broken wrist, three broken ribs, and a dislocated back from troopers' batons, dangers they never considered because of their religious conviction.

chapters.title: Crack Birmingham, Crack the South
chapters.summary: Walker recalls King saying that if they could crack Birmingham they could crack the South, the euphoria of feeling like heavyweight champions, his role as Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington, and how Project C served as the outline for later campaigns with a planned Danville action left unscheduled after King's assassination; he attributes to Birmingham the Public Accommodations Act of 1964 and credits Birmingham with leading to Selma and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

chapters.title: Rallies Were Services
chapters.topic: The Black Church and the Movement
chapters.summary: Walker credits the religious infrastructure of the Black community with everything the movement achieved, describing rallies that were services using century-old spirituals recast with new lyrics as freedom songs, the confederation of Black churches that made the movement possible drawing in adults and young people alike, and King's passing criticism of the preacher in the big Cadillac.

chapters.title: Letter From the Birmingham Jail
chapters.summary: Walker tells how he was the only one in Birmingham who could read King's chicken-scratch writing, transcribing the letter over two or three nights from newspaper edges and toilet paper while his secretary typed to exhaustion, recognizing it as an unusual document he calls the most important of the twentieth century, equal to the Gettysburg Address, and how he renamed the Quakers' first printing from Tears of Love to Letter from the Birmingham Jail.

chapters.title: They Froze My Salary at Ten Thousand
chapters.summary: Walker explains that the SCLC board froze his salary at ten thousand dollars while he administered a million-dollar budget with a wife and four children, so when a New York publisher dangled twenty-five thousand he left, recalling Martin Luther King Sr. coming to his Atlanta home by the little stream to beg him to stay, and his refusal to let Black folks exploit him any more than white folks would.

chapters.title: The Picture in the Birmingham Jail
chapters.summary: Walker says he remained King's man in New York and representative to the UN, recounts going back to jail in Birmingham in 1967 over a parading-without-a-permit case where he and King took each other's photographs, the famous jail picture whose credit others tried to claim, and his Harlem congregation of eighteen hundred members, eighty percent tithing, that gave over a million dollars in his last two years.

chapters.title: The Charter School Fight
chapters.summary: Walker reflects that after King's assassination he felt the church could again lead against the residual racism in cities like New York, becoming immersed in the charter school movement and a central figure who, with Rubén Díaz Sr. and Floyd H. Flake, pushed charter school legislation in New York State and opened the first charter school in the city with Steve Klinsky.

chapters.title: The Struggle on Two Continents
chapters.summary: Walker recounts being recruited by people from the African National Congress to mobilize three hundred churches into a federation to get the truth out about apartheid, working in the election of Mandela as president, taking twenty-two ministers to South Africa to see a racism in which indigenous people could not own land or move freely and had to carry pass books, and casting racism as the outgrowth of hatred against the downtrodden.

chapters.title: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John
chapters.summary: Walker grounds his theological position in the New Testament and the teachings of Jesus, recalling a biographer who, asking who influenced King most and hearing names like Martin Buber, Thoreau, and Paul Tillich, was told by Andrew Young that King was influenced by Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, that King was above all a Christian pastor whose imitation-of-Christ paradigm gave the movement its strength, the beloved community articulated by figures like Vernon Johns and Gardner C. Taylor, and that there is no post-racial era despite Obama's election.

chapters.title: The Invaders and the FBI
chapters.summary: Answering questions from the room, Walker explains that the Memphis march was disrupted by a group called the Invaders who he believed were put up to it by the FBI, that King wanted help straightening it out, that the federal government, particularly the Kennedy administration, repeatedly tried to disrupt the movement, and that King's later focus on jobs grew out of the lack of meaningful employment and poor schools that racism creates.

chapters.part: The Genuine Wife of an Icon
chapters.title: The Truth Is Always Inflammatory
chapters.topic: Telling the Truth and Honoring His Wife
chapters.summary: Walker says his style is to tell the truth, quoting his mentor Vernon Johns that the truth is always inflammatory, and turns to honor his wife as the only genuine wife of a civil rights icon still living, furious that a book on the women of the movement left her out though she was a freedom rider beaten with a rifle butt by an Alabama highwayman, with their home assaulted by guns and rocks in Petersburg.

chapters.part: The Genuine Wife of an Icon
chapters.title: Arrested With Five Children
chapters.summary: Mrs. Walker recounts the 1963 Mother's Day weekend when King asked her husband to stay in Birmingham after a bomb at Dr. King's brother's home set off rioting, her being struck in the head by a National Guardsman at the Gaston Motel while retrieving her sleeping children, then being arrested with her five children at East Point near the Atlanta airport, calling Andrew Young's wife Jean to reach Dr. King, until Martin Luther King Sr. came at midnight with a property owner to make her bail.

chapters.part: The Genuine Wife of an Icon
chapters.title: This Is My Chance to Rest
chapters.topic: The Trial and a Beating at the Motel
chapters.summary: Mrs. Walker describes returning for trial, being fined eighty-four dollars, refusing to pay and going back to a clean jail she treated as a rest until Robert J. Brown paid the fine and her husband, spotting her car, got her out, while Walker recounts being beaten by three highwaymen at the motel as he retrieved her things, suffering a broken wrist, three fractured ribs, and a dislocated spine, a terrible weekend for the family.

chapters.part: The Genuine Wife of an Icon
chapters.title: Homeschooled Before It Was Popular
chapters.topic: Books, a Barred Daughter, and Achievement
chapters.summary: Walker notes his wife went to Mississippi as a freedom rider in a cell of two holding fourteen other women, that she has read all the manuscripts of his fifty-five books, and Mrs. Walker tells how their daughter was barred from all Petersburg public schools over his work as an agitator and president of the Petersburg Improvement Association, CORE, and SCLC, so she homeschooled the girl before homeschooling was popular, the daughter finishing Howard in three years, a Yale master's, and becoming deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts.

chapters.part: The Genuine Wife of an Icon
chapters.title: Too Handsome for Virtue
chapters.summary: Asked whether he would have done anything differently, Walker says no, that he once thought of vengeance but the Lord removed the bitterness and the sixties were an exciting time to be alive, and closes with Maya Angelou, who ran his New York fundraising office, recalling her writing in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings that she saw him with Dr. King and found him too young for wisdom and too handsome for virtue.

loc_video.caption: Wyatt Tee Walker oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Richmond, Virginia, 2014 July 09
